coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and application in Cr2O7 2− detection

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lingling
Wang, Shuai
Xu, Xiuling
Ma, Yongchao
coredata.dc:description: A novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot was synthesized by hydrothermal method using L-phenylalanine and formaldehyde as carbon source, urea as nitrogen source and its structure and morphology were analyzed. Fluorescence analysis experiments show that the quantum dots show good selectivity for Cr2O7 2−, and have strong anti-interference ability, as well as show good linear correlation in the concentration range of 0–300 µL. The quenching mechanism of Cr2O7 2− on CDs is attributed to the internal filtering effect (IFE). CDs is expected to be applied to the detection of industrial wastewater in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped
Carbon Quantum Dots
Cr2O7 2−
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV and FL analysis of CDs
Fluorescence intensity analysis at different concentration
Fluorescence stability analysis of carbon quantum dot
Influence of other metal ions on selectivity and detection sensitivity of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV absorption spectrum and (B) fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs. The inset is photograph of CDs under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp.
(A) TEM (B) particle size distribution (C) EDS electronic images (D) EDS element content of CDs.
XRD spectrum (A) and Infrared spectrum (B) of CDs.
(A) XPS spectra and corresponding expansion for (B) C1s, (C) N1s and (D) O1s peaks of CDs.
(A) XRD of CDs soaked in different pH for 12 h (B) TGA curve of CDs.
Fluorescence intensity of CDs at (A) different concentrations and (B) different pH.
Fluorescence intensity of CDs under (A) different UV irradiation time and (B) different NaCl concentration.
Fluorescence intensity of CDs interacting with (A) different cations and (B) different anion.
Variation of fluorescence intensity of different cations before (black) and after (red) mixing with Cr2O7 2−.
Effect of Cr2O7 2− concentration on fluorescence quenching of CDs; (A) fluorescence intensity (B) linear relationship.
(A) Fluorescence lifetime of CDs and CDs + Cr2O7 2− (B) Normalized UV absorption spectra of Cr2O7 2− and normalized fluorescence emission spectra of CDs.
Schematic diagram of preparation of CDs and detection of Cr2O7 2−.
Quantum yields of CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and application in Cr2O7 2− detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food, Haidu College, Qingdao Agricultural University, Yantai 265200 PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food, Haidu College, Qingdao Agricultural University, Yantai 265200, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chengyang Branch of Qingdao Ecological Environment Bureau, Qingdao 266109 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chengyang Branch of Qingdao Ecological Environment Bureau

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chengyang Branch of Qingdao Ecological Environment Bureau, Qingdao 266109, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot was synthesized by hydrothermal method using L-phenylalanine and formaldehyde as carbon source, urea as nitrogen source and its structure and morphology were analyzed. Fluorescence analysis experiments show that the quantum dots show good selectivity for Cr2O7 2−, and have strong anti-interference ability, as well as show good linear correlation in the concentration range of 0–300 µL. The quenching mechanism of Cr2O7 2− on CDs is attributed to the internal filtering effect (IFE). CDs is expected to be applied to the detection of industrial wastewater in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cr2O7 2− ions usually come from the production process of metallurgy, metal plating, steel manufacturing, leather, metal deep processing, textile industry and other industries, and enter the water system through industrial emissions, becoming a common water pollutant [1–3]. Compared with other heavy metal ions, Cr2O7 2− ions are very soluble in water over almost the entire pH range and are easily transported in groundwater systems. Cr2O7 2− ions in industrial wastewater usually enrich into human body through water system or food chain, causing irreversible damage [4,5]. Studies have shown that Cr2O7 2− ions has serious toxic hazards, which has carcinogenicity, its strong oxidation can damage DNA and interfere with DNA repair, at the same time it can also damage the liver cell skeleton, and even lead to liver cell death [6,7]. In addition, it can also cause nephrotoxicity, reproductive toxicity, genetic toxicity and so on, which is listed as a level human carcinogen by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), so accurate detection of Cr2O7 2− in water is very important for the environment and health [8–10].

At present, the determination methods of Cr2O7 2− reported in the literature include ultraviolet spectrophotometry, ion exchange method, fluorescence spectrometry, voltammetry, atomic absorption spectrometry, adsorbent adsorption and photocatalytic degradation method [11,12]. Among these methods, fluorescence spectrometry has attracted much attention because of its high sensitivity, good selectivity, fast response and simple operation, and is the most promising technology [13–15]. However, Cr2O7 2− itself does not have fluorescence, so it cannot be directly detected by fluorescence spectroscopy, and it needs to be indirectly detected by fluorescent probe [16,17].

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have been successfully applied in fields of analysis and detection, optoelectronics, biology and chemistry due to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, low cost and unique photoelectric transmission [18–21]. The application of CDs in analysis and detection is mainly based on their excellent fluorescence properties, such as strong fluorescence, good optical stability, large extinction coefficient, wide absorption spectrum and narrow fluorescence spectrum [22–24]. In the detection of heavy metal ions, the mechanism of CDs detecting heavy metal ions is that the functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl on the surface of the CDs coordinate or complex with the metal ions, thus the fluorescence of the CDs is quenched or enhanced, and finally the qualitative or quantitative analysis of substances to be detected is realized [25–27].

L-Phenylalanine, Urea, Formaldehyde, CuCl2·6H2O, CdCl2·2H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, KI, K2Cr2O7, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, KF, KIO3, K2CrO4, NiCl2·6H2O, CaCl2, K2SO4, FeCl3·6H2O, NaCl, Al(NO3)3, Cr(NO3)3, KBr, NaNO3 and other reagents were purchased from Aladin Reagent (Shanghai) Co Ltd. without further purification before use.

High resolution transmission electron microscope (F200), Nippon Electronics Co Ltd; Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicoleti S10), Thermo Fisher, USA; Fluorescence spectrometer (F7000), Hitachi, Japan; UV–Vis spectrometer (V-3900), Hitachi, Japan; Nanometer particle size analyzer (Zse), Malvern, UK; X-ray diffractometer (D8ADVANCE), Bruker AG, Germany; Scanning electron microscope and energy dispersive spectrometer (JSM-IT500), Japan Electronics, Japan; X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (SCIENTIFIC K-ALPHA), Thermo Fisher, USA.

L-Phenylalanine (0.115 mmol, 0.019 g), urea (5 mmol, 0.3 g) and formaldehyde (560 µL) were placed in a beaker, 5 mL of distilled water was added and stirred vigorously for 2 h. Then transferred to a reaction kettle with polytetrafluoroethylene liner, heated at 220℃ for 10 h, and then naturally cooled to room temperature, the obtained mixture solution was filtered through a water system 0.22 mm filter membrane, and then dialyzed with dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off = 1000), and the water was changed every two hours to remove unreacted small molecules. And finally freeze-drying the obtained solution to obtain the CDs.

The morphology of the samples was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy; The structure of the CDs is analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer; Analyzing the size of the CDs by laser particle size analyzer; In addition, the optical properties of the CDs are analyzed by an ultraviolet spectrophotometer and a fluorescence spectrophotometer; Diffraction analysis of the CDs was carried out by X-ray diffraction analysis, and the diffraction range was 5-90°, and other tests not specified were carried out by conventional means; HRTEM observation of CDs morphology; X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used to record the element analysis results.

Solution preparation: firstly, preparing 0.1 mM of K2Cr2O7, CuCl2·6H2O, CdCl2·2H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, KI, K2CrO4, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, KF, KIO3, NiCl2·6H2O, CaCl2, K2SO4, FeCl3·6H2O, NaCl, Al(NO3)3, Cr(NO3)3, KBr, NaNO3 solution. The optimal concentration at the excitation wavelength was determined as 0.10 mg·mL−1, 0.20 mg·mL−1, 0.30 mg·mL−1, 0.40 mg·mL−1, 0.50 mg·mL−1, 0.60 mg·mL−1, 0.70 mg·mL−1, 0.80 mg·mL−1, 0.90 mg·mL−1 and 1.00 mg·mL−1 carbon quantum dot solution were stirred uniformly at room temperature and incubated for 3 min, and then that fluorescence spectra were teste to select the optimal excitation wavelength.

Test the standard curve: after determining the concentration corresponding to the optimal excitation wavelength according to the above experiment, add 0.1 mM K2Cr2O7 solution into the test system in turn, add 30 µL each time and then incubate at room temperature for 3 min for fluorescence test, stop add until the fluorescence intensity does not change significantly, record and analyze the data.

Selectivity test: add 0.1 mM cations (Cu2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, K+, Fe3+, Na+, Al3+, Cr3+), anions (I−, Cr2O7 2−, F−, IO3 −, SO4 2−, Cl−, Br−, NO3 −, CrO4 2−) and blank control 100 µL into 2 mL of carbon quantum dot solution with the optimal concentration determined above. Other conditions are consistent with the standard curve test.

Interference test: for the interference test, pipetting 100 µL of interfering ion solution (I−, Cr2O7 2−, F−, IO3 −, SO4 2−, Cl−, Br−, NO3 −, CrO4 2−) having a concentration of 0.1 mM to the above solution containing 100 μL Cr2O7 2− solution test system, other conditions and the standard curve test consistent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV and FL analysis of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in the illustration, the prepared CDs show light yellow under natural light and bright blue under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, and have strong fluorescence. Fig. 1 (A) shows the UV absorption spectrum of CDs, which have a wide UV absorption range between 200–350 nm, providing a basis for the selection of excitation wavelengths. Fig. 1 (B) is the fluorescence spectrum under different excitation wavelengths (246 nm, 256 nm, 266 nm, 276 nm, 286 nm, 286 nm, 306 nm, 316 nm, 326 nm, 336 nm and 346 nm). The CDs have strong fluorescence intensity. Table 1 shows the quantum yield of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. Through comparison, it was found that the quantum yield at 256 nm-336 nm showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing. When the quantum yield reaches a maximum of 6.63 % at an excitation wavelength of 296 nm, it approaches the optimal excitation wavelength, resulting in excited molecules with higher energy conversion efficiency and quantum yield. Therefore, the excitation wavelength of 296 nm was used in the subsequent experiments.

The transmission electron microscope of the CDs is shown in Fig. 2 (A), and it can be seen from the figure that the appearance of the quantum dots is spherical particles with good monodispersity and no agglomeration. Fig. 2 (B) is an average particle size distribution diagram of the CDs, the particle size range is about 3.0–6.0 nm, and the average particle size is 4.5 nm, which is basically consistent with the data obtained by the transmission electron microscope, so we can prove that the CDs obtained by the hydrothermal method are indeed CDs. Fig. S1 (A) shows the SEM image of CDs, which have a large size. This may be due to the requirement of SEM testing that CDs agglomerate during the sample preparation process. And Fig. S1 (B) shows the HRTEM image of CDs, which founds that and found that the lattice spacing is 0.22 nm. Fig. 2 (C) and (D) correspond to the EDS electronic images and elemental content of CDs. From the electronic images, it can be seen that C, O, and N elements are evenly distributed in CDs, with elemental contents of 69.51 %, 18.18 %, and 12.31 %, respectively.

The crystal structure of the CDs is characterized through X-ray diffraction test, the result is as shown in Fig. 3 (A), a broad peak appears at 24°, the curve is not smooth and the data analysis is corresponding to the (002) crystal face of graphite carbon, which proves that the carbon quantum dot is an amorphous structure and has sp2 hybrid carbon core of graphite carbon.

As shown in Fig. 3 (B), there are a hydroxyl characteristic absorption peak at 3277 cm−1; 1675 cm−1 belong to C=O stretching vibration peak; The bending vibration peaks of –OH and C–H are at 1384 cm−1 and 1126 cm−1. The N–H stretching vibration of CDs at 3437 cm−1 is caused by water heat in urea; The absorption at 2926 cm−1, 1601 cm−1, 1384 cm−1 and 1126 cm−1 corresponds to C–H, C=O, C-N and C-O-C stretching vibration, respectively. The results showed that the surface of the prepared CDs was rich in oxygen-containing functional groups such as –NH2, −C=O, –OH, which improved the hydrophilicity and stability of CDs, and showed electronegativity in aqueous solution.

In order to further confirm the surface composition and chemical environment of CDs, XPS testing was conducted on CDs and spectra were obtained. Fig. 4 (A) shows three prominent peaks at 284.82 eV, 399.51 eV, and 531.60 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The proportion of three elements in CDs is C = 72.16 %, N = 11.15 %, and O = 16.69 %, which is similar to the element proportion results obtained from EDS testing. The high-resolution C1s peak shown in Fig. 4 (B) was deconvoluted into three peaks, with 284.73 eV corresponding to C–C and C–H, 286.19 eV corresponding to C-N and C-O bonds, and 288.11 eV corresponding to C=O. Fig. 4 (C) shows that N1s has also been deconvolved to 399.49 eV and 402.41 eV corresponding to C-N and N–H. Fig. 4 (D) shows that the high-resolution O1s spectrum is deconvoluted to 531.52 eV, 532.32 eV, and 533.62 eV, respectively, corresponding to C=O, C-O, O–H. The XPS results indicate the successful synthesis of CDs, and the results are consistent with the FRIT analysis results. After solvent thermal reaction, abundant functional groups were generated on the surface.

To test the pH stability of CDs, they were soaked in aqueous solutions with pH = 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 for 12 h, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 ℃. Perform XRD testing after drying. In Fig. 5 (A), compared with untreated CDs, the peak shape and peak position after soaking in different pH solutions did not change, indicating that CDs have good acid-base stability. Fig. 5 (B) shows the TGA curve of CDs, which shows that weight loss begins at 210 ℃ and stabilizes at 470 ℃. This can be attributed to the loss of water molecules on the surface of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence intensity analysis at different concentration
ce:section.ce:para: At the excitation wavelength of 296 nm, we measured the fluorescence intensity at different concentrations of 0.05 mg mL−1, 0.10 mg mL−1, 0.15 mg mL−1, 0.20 mg mL−1, 0.25 mg mL−1, 0.30 mg mL−1, 0.35 mg mL−1, 0.40 mg mL−1, 0.45 mg mL−1, 0.50 mg mL−1, 0.55 mg mL−1, 0.60 mg mL−1, 0.65 mg mL−1, 0.70 mg mL−1. As shown in Fig. 6 (A), the fluorescence intensity increases gradually during the process of increasing the concentration from 0.05 mg mL−1 to 0.30 mg mL−1. However, when the concentration continues to increase, the fluorescence intensity decreases significantly during the process of increasing from 0.30 mg mL−1 to 0.70 mg mL−1. As the density of CDs in the solution increases, collisions between monomers and solvent molecules become more frequent, which may consume some of the energy in the process and lead to a decrease in fluorescence intensity. Therefore, the high concentration range is not suitable for fluorescence detection and does not conform to the principle of high sensitivity. Thus, we chose 0.30 mg mL−1 as the best test concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence stability analysis of carbon quantum dot
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 (B) shows the change of the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in the range of pH 1–12. In the neutral environment, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs is stable, while in the strong acidic (pH = 1–4) or strong alkaline solution (pH = 12), the fluorescence intensity of the CDs changes greatly. This is because the surface of the quantum dots is functional groups such as –NH2 and –COOH. In the strong acid or strong alkaline solution, the functional groups are no longer stable, and deprotonation occurs, so that the mother nucleus is no longer stable. But it has strong stability in a wide pH range (pH = 5–11), which provides a foundation for application in organisms such as imaging.

In order to explore the anti UV bleaching performance of CDs, the CDs solution were placed in a 365 nm UV lamp and every 15 min to measure their fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. 7 (A), with the increase of time, even when the ultraviolet irradiation time reaches 120 min, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs does not change significantly. Therefore, it can be proved that the CDs have good UV bleaching resistance.

Finally, we investigated the effect of different salt concentrations on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. As shown in Fig. 7 (B), the fluorescence intensity of CDs changes significantly in the range of NaCl concentration of 0.4–2.8 M, indicating that the fluorescence performance of CDs did change significantly in the high-intensity ionic environment, so it cannot provide a good basis for practical applications under different physiological conditions.

Fig. 8 (A) is the fluorescence intensity diagram under the action of different metal cations Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, and (B) anions SO4 2−, Br−, F−, I−, IO3 −, Cl−, NO3 −, CrO4 2−and blank, the concentration of metal ions is 0.1 mM, it can be seen that the fluorescence quenching effect of Cr2O7 2− on CDs is the strongest, and the quenching rate is about 58.49 %. While other cations and anions have little effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs, which proves that CDs have better selectivity for Cr2O7 2−.

The possible mechanism of Cr2O7 2− quenching the fluorescence of CDs is speculated as follows: the force between –NH2 and –COOH passivated on the surface of CDs and Cr2O7 2− is stronger than that of other ions, which increases the defects on the surface of CDs, suppresses the non-radiative jump and leads to the fluorescence quenching of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of other metal ions on selectivity and detection sensitivity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, we also investigated the influence of the presence of other metal ions on the detection of Cr2O7 2− by CDs. As shown in Fig. 9 , when the same concentration of 0.1 mM of other anions (I−, F−, IO3 −, SO4 2−, Cl−, Br−, NO3 −, CrO4 2−) and blank, measure their fluorescence intensity (black column), and then add Cr2O7 2− solution of the same concentration to test their fluorescence intensity (red column). The results show that the presence of other metal ions does not affect the quenching effect of Cr2O7 2− on the CDs, which proves that the CDs have specificity for the detection of Cr2O7 2−.

Fig. 10 shows the quenching of the fluorescence intensity of CDs by different concentrations of Cr2O7 2−. It can be seen from Fig. 8. A that the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually decreases with the increase of Cr2O7 2− concentration and presents a linear change. Fig. 10 (B) shows the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CDs and the concentration of Cr2O7 2−. Within the concentration range of 0–300 µL, the linear relationship between Cr2O7 2− and fluorescence intensity is F = 1205.44667–2.86689C, and the linear correlation coefficient is 0.96621. Substitute the data and convert to obtain quenching constant Ksv = 1.37 × 105 M−1 and the detection limit LOD = 0.68 μM. Therefore, it is proved that CDs have good detection sensitivity for Cr2O7 2−, which provides a possibility for the detection of actual water samples in the future.

Firstly, in order to explore the mechanism of CDs for fluorescence detection of Cr2O7 2−, fluorescence lifetime tests were conducted on the CDs testing system before and after adding Cr2O7 2−. Fig. 11 (A) shows that the fluorescence lifetime of the test system before Cr2O7 2− addition is 2.01 ns, and after Cr2O7 2− addition is 2.03 ns, with no significant change between the two. This indicates that the quenching of CDs by Cr2O7 2− does not belong to dynamic quenching. Then, solid UV absorption tests were conducted on Cr2O7 2−. In Fig. 11 (B), it was found that the UV absorption spectrum of Cr2O7 2− covered the emission spectrum of CDs, indicating a competitive absorption relationship between the two. In summary, the quenching mechanism of Cr2O7 2− on CDs is attributed to the internal filtering effect (IFE).

In this paper, the novel CDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method using L-phenylalanine and formaldehyde as carbon source, urea as nitrogen source and distilled water as solvent. It was proved that the QDs were uniform spherical structure with a diameter of about 4.5 nm and amorphous carbon structure. And that surface is rich in functional group such as –NH2, –COOH and has good hydrophilicity. The fluorescence properties of CDs were investigated by UV and fluorescence spectrum. Due to the good quenching effect of Cr2O7 2− on CDs, a fluorescence method for the determination of Cr2O7 2− was established. The quenching mechanism of Cr2O7 2− on CDs is attributed to the internal filtering effect (IFE). The experimental results show that the CDs have good selectivity for Cr2O7 2− and strong anti-interference ability. The fluorescence intensity was linear with the concentration of Cr2O7 2− in the range of 0–300 µL. The CDs have good selectivity and anti-interference for Cr2O7 2− detection, have potential application prospect in ion analysis and detection, and are expected to be applied to the detection of actual water samples in the future.
Lingling Zhang: Writing – original draft. Shuai Wang: Writing – original draft. Xiuling Xu: Writing – review & editing. Yongchao Ma: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (ZR2021QE064), the Research Foundation for Distinguished Scholars of Qingdao Agricultural University (665-1120018, 663-1120082) and Central Leading Local Science and Technology Development Special Plan Project (24-2-4-2-zyyd-nsh).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2025.114023.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
